complemented by the Speaker who heads the House of Representatives.

items.question: How many Senators make up the Nigerian Senate?
items.explanation:
The Senate consists of three Senators from each State and one from the FCT, 
making a total of

items.question: 8. What is the role of the Legislature regarding laws made by the government?
options: It makes the laws but does not implement them.
items.explanation:
Though the Legislature approves the Budget and makes the laws, it does not 
implement such laws. That duty belongs to the Executive.

items.question:
Which of these is an exclusive responsibility of the Federal Government, according to the 
handbook?
items.explanation:
The Federal Government has exclusive responsibility for specified matters such 
as Foreign Affairs.

items.question:
Matters on which both the Federal and State Governments share responsibilities are listed 
in which part of the Constitution?
items.explanation:
These areas which appear in the Concurrent List in the Constitution, include 
Health, Education, Roads etc..

items.question: How many Local Governments are there in Nigeria?
items.explanation:
There exists, in the country, a third tier of Government called the Local 
Government of which there are 774 in all, across the nation.
373

items.question: Who is the topmost career Civil Servant in a Local Government set-up?
items.explanation: The topmost career Civil Servant in the set-up is designated as Secretary

items.question: What is the duty and responsibility of the Executive Council of the Federation?
options: To determine Government policies on various matters.
To confirm appointments of Ministers and Ambassadors.

To manage the daily affairs of the Judiciary.
□
items.explanation:
The Executive Council of the Federation has the duty and responsibility of 
determining Government policies on various matters.

items.question: Who is the Secretary to the Executive Council of the Federation?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
The Secretary to the Government of the Federation

The Minister of Finance
Answer: C
items.explanation:
The Secretary to the Council is the Secretary to the Government of the 
Federation.

items.question: What is the designation of the Chief Executive of a Ministry of Cabinet rank?
items.explanation: Each Ministry is headed by a Minister of Cabinet rank who is the Chief Executive.

items.question: Who is the Chief Adviser to the Minister and also the Ministry's Accounting Officer?
options: The Chairman of the Extra-Ministerial Department
The Head of the Civil Service of the Federationa 374
items.explanation:
His Chief Adviser is the Permanent Secretary who is also the Ministry's 
Accounting Officer.

items.question:
Which of the following is explicitly mentioned as part of the Public Service, apart from the 
Civil Service?
items.explanation:
Apart from the Civil Service, the Public Service also encompasses the Armed 
Forces, the Judiciary, the Legislature and the Police..

items.question:
What characteristic of good governance emphasizes fair legal frameworks that are enforced 
impartially?
items.explanation:
Good governance comprises characteristics such as "Rule of Law," which 
includes fair legal frameworks that are enforced impartially..

items.question:
What does the "Transparency" characteristic of good governance imply regarding 
information?
options: Information is strictly controlled and limited.
Information is freely available and accessible to those who will be affected by decisions.
Information is only shared with government officials.
Decisions are taken without explaining the reasons.a
items.explanation:
Under Transparency, information is freely available and accessible to those who 
will be affected by such decisions and their enforcement.

items.question:
What characteristic of good governance requires governmental institutions, the private 
sector, and civil society organizations to be accountable to the public?
items.explanation:
Under "Accountability," governmental institutions as well as the private sector and 
civil society organizations must be accountable to the public and to their institutional 
stakeholders.
Pat 2: Civil Service Handbook: Government Revenue and Expenditure

items.question: What is another term for "Public Finance," as defined in the Civil Service Handbook?
items.explanation:
The term "Public Finance" is also referred to as "Government Finance" or "Public 
Moneys"

items.question:
According to Section 2 of the Finance (Control and Management) Act of 1958, what does 
"Public Finance" include?
options: Only the Public Revenue of the Federation
Any moneys held by a private citizen

The Public Revenue of the Federation and any moneys held in an official capacity by a Public 
Service Officer or Government agent
items.explanation:
Public Finance includes "The Public Revenue of the Federation" and "Any monies 
held in his official capacity... by any Officer in the Public Service of the Federation or by any 
agent of the government".

items.question:
Modern-day government intervenes in the economy primarily through what types of 
policies?
items.explanation:
The interest of the modern-day government in the economy is manifested via 
intervention through fiscal and monetary policies.

items.question: Government revenue can be broadly classified into which two sections?
items.explanation:
Government revenue "can broadly be classified into two sections, namely: (a) Oil 
Revenue, (b) Non-Oil Revenue”

items.question: Which of the following is an example of Oil Revenue?
items.explanation:
OIL REVENUE includes Petroleum Profit Tax, Rent Royalties and Government 
Crude Oil Sales, and proceeds from domestic consumption

items.question: Which of these is explicitly mentioned as a component of Non-Oil Revenue?
options:
Proceeds from domestic consumption of oil.
□
items.explanation:
Non-Oil Revenue refers to Indirect and Direct Taxes, Company Tax, Income Tax, 
Personal Income tax, etc

items.question: Non-tax revenue refers to income accruable to the government outside of its taxes, such as:
items.explanation:
The non-tax revenue refers to income accruable to the government outside its 
taxes such as operating surplus from its parastatals.

items.question:
Which body is responsible for monitoring the accruals to and disbursement of revenue from 
the Federation Account?
options: The Revenue Mobilization Allocation and Fiscal Commission
items.explanation:
The Revenue Mobilization Allocation and Fiscal Commission is one of the Federal 
Executive Bodies... Among other functions, the Commission has the power to: monitor the 
accruals to and disbursement of revenue from the Federation Account

items.question:
What percentage of the revenue allocation formula currently in operation goes to the 
Federal Government?
items.explanation:
The Revenue Allocation formula in operation among the three tiers of government 
is: Federal Government 52.68%

items.question: What percentage of the Federal Government's share of derivation goes to Ecology?
items.explanation: FGN Share of Derivation Ecology is 1.00% of the Federal Government's share

items.question:
Which account is a distributable pooling account where all revenues collected by the 
Federal Government are paid, with certain exceptions?
items.explanation:
The Federation Account is a distributable (special) pooling account in which all 
revenues collected by the Federal Government are paid except for certain specified proceeds

items.question: The Consolidated Revenue Fund is solely for which level of government?
items.explanation:
The Consolidated Revenue Fund... is not distributable, but solely for the Federal
378
Government

items.question: Which of these is a source of revenue for the Consolidated Revenue Fund?
options: Proceeds of tax under pay-as-you-earn system from police personnel
Proceeds from domestic consumption of crude oil
items.explanation:
Sources of revenue for the Consolidated Revenue Fund include "Share from the 
Federation Account”.

items.question: What type of expenditures are charged to the Consolidated Revenue Fund?
items.explanation:
All Recurrent Expenditures including the salaries of the Auditor-General, Chief 
Justice, President etc are charged to it

items.question: The Development Fund is primarily meant for what type of projects?
items.explanation: This Fund is meant for capital projects. It is established by the 1999 Constitution.

items.question: What is the purpose of the Contingency Fund?
options: To take care of unforeseen circumstances such as national disasters.
To distribute revenue among the tiers of government, a
items.explanation:
The Contingency Fund was established to take care of un-foreseen 
circumstances such as national disasters, fire-outbreaks, serious flooding etc.80.

items.question:
The responsibility for the Federal Budget is shared between which two arms of the Federal 
Government?
options:
The President and the Chief Justice
Q
items.explanation:
The responsibility for the Federal Budget is shared between the Executive and 
the Legislature, which are two of the three arms of the Federal Government

items.question: Who is responsible for preparing the Federal Budget?
items.explanation:
Therefore, the Executive is responsible for preparing the Federal Budget while the 
Legislature approves it

items.question:
What does the President's directives to the Minister of Finance and the Budget Office of the 
Federation aim to achieve through the budget?
options: To reduce poverty, generate wealth and create jobs
items.explanation:
The plans indicate what the Government intends to do to reduce poverty, 
generate wealth and create jobs

items.question:
What serves as a policy tool for attaining the immediate, medium and long-term 
development goals of the Government?
items.explanation:
As the preparation of the Federal Budget is done against the background of these 
development plans, the budget serves as a policy tool for attaining the immediate, medium and 
long-term development goals of Government

items.question:
Who projects estimates of oil and non-oil revenues in consultation with revenue generating 
agencies?
options: The Budget Office of the Federation
items.explanation:
The Budget Office of the Federation, in consultation with the revenue generating 
agencies of the Federal Government, projects estimates of oil and non-oil revenues

items.question:
How many years are the revenues estimated for and documented in the Medium-Term 
Revenue Framework?
items.explanation:
These revenues are estimated for the next three years and are documented in 
the Medium-Term Revenue Framework

items.question:
What is the aim when determining the estimates for the Medium-Term Expenditure 
Framework?
options: To maximize government spending regardless of revenue
To prioritize short-term gains over long-term goals

To balance the need to spend money for developmental goals and the need to live within our 
means

To only spend money from external grants
□
items.explanation:
In determining these estimates, the aim is to balance the need to spend money to 
attain the nation's developmental goals and the need to live within our means

items.question: What fiscal rule is observed under the Fiscal Responsibility Act 2007?
options: Total revenue must always exceed total expenditure.
Total expenditure must not exceed total revenue.
Total expenditure can exceed total revenue if there are external grants.
Total revenue is not a factor in determining total expenditure.a
items.explanation:
The fiscal rule being observed under the Fiscal Responsibility Act 2007 is that 
total expenditure must not exceed total revenue

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an arm of Government mentioned in Chapter 1?
items.explanation:
Government is made up of three arms, viz; the Legislature, the Executive and the 
Judiciary. The Civil Service is part of the Public Service, which encompasses the three arms of 
Government

items.question: Who heads the Judiciary at the Federal level?
options: The Speaker of the House of Representatives
items.explanation: The Judiciary is headed by the Chief Justice of Nigeria

items.question:
What is the primary duty of the Executive arm of Government regarding policies and 
programs?
items.explanation:
The Executive is responsible for the day-to-day management of Government. In 
this context, it conceives, formulates, executes and monitors the policies, programmes and 
projects of the Government.

items.question:
Which list in the Constitution contains matters where both Federal and State Governments 
share responsibilities?
items.explanation:
These areas which appear in the Concurrent List in the Constitution, include 
Health, Education, Roads etc

items.question: How many members are in the House of Representatives?
items.explanation: The House of Representatives consists of 360 members

items.question: What is the lowest tier of Government mentioned in the handbook?
items.explanation:
There exists, in the country, a third tier of Government called the Local 
Government of which there are 774 in all, across the nation

items.question: What is the designation of Chief Executives for Extra-Ministerial Departments?
items.explanation:
Extra-Ministerial Departments, on the other hand, usually have their Chief 
Executives designated as Chairmen e.g. Chairman, Federal Civil Service Commission.

items.question: Besides the Civil Service, which of the following is explicitly part of the Public Service?
items.explanation:
Apart from the Civil Service, the Public Service also encompasses the Armed 
Forces, the Judiciary, the Legislature and the Police166.
383

items.question:
Which characteristic of good governance implies that institutions and processes produce 
results that meet societal needs while making the best use of resources?
items.explanation:
Effectiveness and Efficiency: processes and institutions produce results that 
meet the needs of society while making the best use of resources at their disposal

items.question: 56. What is a key aspect of "Equity and Inclusiveness" in good governance?
options: Ensuring only certain groups benefit from development
Ensuring all members of society feel they have a stake and are not excluded

Prioritizing the needs of the majority over minorities
D Discriminating based on religion or ethnic group
□
items.explanation:
Equity and Inclusiveness: ensuring that all members of society feel that they 
have a stake in it and do not feel excluded from the mainstream.

items.question: What does "Consensus Orientation" in good governance emphasize?
options:
The need for strict adherence to a single viewpoint
B.The need for mediation of different interests to reach broad consensus for the whole 
community's best interest
items.explanation:
Consensus Orientation: need for mediation of the different interests in the 
society to reach a broad consensus on what is in the best interest of the whole community and 
how this can be achieved
384

items.question:
What characteristic of good governance focuses on institutions and processes serving all 
stakeholders within a reasonable timeframe?
items.explanation:
Responsiveness: institutions and processes try to serve all stakeholders within a 
reasonable timeframe

items.question: What is the role of the Civil Service in relation to good governance, as stated in Chapter 1?
options: To hinder the implementation of government policies

To play a crucial role in ensuring Nigeria is administered consistent with good governance 
attributes
To only monitor, not actively participate in governance a
items.explanation:
The civil service has a crucial role to play in ensuring that Nigeria is administered 
in a manner consistent with the aforementioned attributes and characteristics of good 
governance

items.question: What is NOT a characteristic of good governance listed in the handbook?
items.explanation:
Chapter 10 of the handbook lists several characteristics of good governance, 
including Rule of Law, Participation, Transparency, Accountability, Effectiveness and Efficiency, 
Equity and Inclusiveness, Consensus Orientation, Responsiveness, Security and Order, 
Conflicts (capacity to manage), Purposeful Leadership, and Welfare of People. Autocracy is not 
listed and is contrary to the principles of good governance.
Part 3: Civil Service Handbook: Due Process and Bureau of Public Procurement

items.question:
The Due Process policy is described as a novel anti-corruption mechanism of the Federal 
Government aimed at providing transparency and accountability in what specific areas?
options: Public sector procurement, government budgetary, and financial operations
Judicial processes and legal frameworks a 385
items.explanation:
The Due Process policy's aim is to provide transparency and accountability in 
procurement and in government budgetary and financial operations.

items.question:
The concept of "Due Process" applies to which of the following aspects of government 
business?
options: All aspects of Government business, including negotiation, recruitment, and promotion
items.explanation:
A common error is associating "due process" only with procurement, but it applies 
to all other aspects of government business, such as negotiation, recruitment, and promotion.

items.question: What is one of the main objectives of the Due Process policy?
options: To increase government spending on all projects
To strictly enforce due process principles of transparency, competition, and efficiency
To promote extra-budgetary spending in Ministries, Departments, and Agencies (MDAs)
To centralize all procurement decisions at the federal level a .
items.explanation:
A main objective is to strictly enforce due process principles of transparency, 
competition, efficiency, and value for money in the procurement of public goods, works, and 
services.

items.question:
According to a 1999-2000 Country Procurement Assessment Report, how much of every N1 
spent by the government was lost to underhand practices?
items.explanation:
A report from 1999-2000 revealed that 60k was lost to underhand practices out of 
every N1 spent by the Government.

items.question:
The Bureau for Monitoring and Price Intelligence Unit (BMPIU) was set up in which year to 
address procurement shortcomings?
A.1999
items.explanation:
The Bureau for Monitoring and Price Intelligence Unit (BMPIU) was established in 
2001 to address the identified shortcomings in procurement.

items.question: The Public Procurement Act was signed into law on which date?
items.explanation:
The Public Procurement Act 2007 was signed into law on June 4, 2007, by the 
President.

items.question: What is the essential objective of the Public Procurement Act 2007?
options:
To establish the National Council on Public Procurement and the Bureau of Public 
Procurement as regulatory authorities
To increase the government's budget deficit

To remove all existing procurement policies and practices
□ .
items.explanation:
The essential objective of the Public Procurement Act 2007 is the "Establishment 
of National Council on Public Procurement and the Bureau of Public Procurement as the 
regulatory authorities responsible for the monitoring and oversight of public procurement".

items.question:
The Public Procurement Act 2007 applies to entities that derive at least what percentage of 
funds for any procurement from the Federation Share of Consolidated Revenue Fund?
items.explanation:
The Act applies to entities that derive at least 35% of funds appropriated for any 
procurement from the Federation Share of Consolidated Revenue Fund.

items.question:
The Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP) formulates general policies and guidelines for 
public sector procurement for the approval of which body?
items.explanation:
One of the main functions of the Bureau is to formulate general policies and 
guidelines relating to public sector procurement for the approval of the Council.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Bureau of Public Procurement?
options: Publicizing and explaining the provisions of the Act
Certifying federal procurement prior to contract awards

Publishing the details of major contracts in the procurement journal
□ .
items.explanation:
The functions of the Bureau include publicizing the Act, certifying procurement, 
and publishing contract details, but not directly implementing projects.

items.question:
The Bureau of Public Procurement is responsible for maintaining a national database of 
what?
items.explanation:
The Bureau's functions include monitoring the prices of tendered items and 
keeping a national database of standard process prices.

items.question:
How often is the Bureau of Public Procurement required to submit procurement audits to the 
National Assembly?
items.explanation:
The Bureau is required to perform procurement audits and submit such reports to 
the National Assembly bi-annually.

items.question:
What is the purpose of the single internet portal to be established by the Bureau of Public 
Procurement?
options: To serve as a private communication channel for government officials
To serve as a primary and definitive source of all information on government procurement
To be a platform for public complaints only
items.explanation:
The Bureau is to establish a single internet portal that will serve as a primary and
388
definitive source of all information on government procurement.

items.question: What does the Bureau do to ensure compliance with the principles of due process?
options: It holds regular meetings with the private sector.
It reviews and certifies Federal Government contracts.
It lobbies the National Assembly for more funding.
It prosecutes all cases of contract inflation.a .
items.explanation:
The Bureau reviews and certifies Federal Government contracts according to 
established thresholds to ensure compliance with due process principles.

items.question:
According to the fundamental principles for procurements, no procurement proceedings 
shall be formalized until the procuring entity has done what?
options: Ensured that funds are available to meet the obligations
Secured approval from the Minister of Finance

Received a 'No Objection' certificate from the President
□ .
items.explanation:
No procurement proceedings shall be formalized until the procuring entity has 
ensured that funds are available to meet the obligations.

items.question:
What must a procuring entity obtain from the Bureau before a contract can be awarded, 
subject to the regulations?
options: A 'No Objection' to Contract Award certificate
A signed agreement from all biddersa .
items.explanation:
The procuring entity must obtain a certificate of 'No Objection' to Contract Award 
from the Bureau.

items.question:
The Public Procurement Act requires all public procurement to be conducted using what 
bidding method?
items.explanation:
All public procurement should be conducted by open competitive bidding.
389

items.question:
Due Process is aimed at ensuring fiscal transparency and strict compliance with procedures 
in which areas?
items.explanation:
The Due Process is aimed at ensuring fiscal transparency and strict compliance 
20 with procedures, effectiveness, and efficiency in the costing of projects and services .

items.question: The Public Procurement Act 2007 institutionalized the operations of which unit?
options: The Bureau for Monitoring and Price Intelligence Unit (BMPIU)
The Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation

The National Assembly Budget and Research Office
Q .
items.explanation:
The steps to institutionalize the operations of the Bureau for Monitoring and Price 
Intelligence Unit (BMPIU) culminated in the signing into law of the Public Procurement Act 2007.

items.question: Which principle of public procurement is emphasized to prevent contract inflation?
options: To ensure cost reasonableness and accuracy
To guarantee elimination of waste a .
items.explanation:
The objective to prevent contract inflation is achieved by ensuring cost 
reasonableness, accuracy, and comparability of all Public contracts with national, regional, and 
global costs.

items.question: What is the purpose of certifying projects for public funding under the Due Process policy?
options: To fast-track projects without proper scrutiny
To fund projects that have not been included in the budget

To certify only those projects and contracts that pass proper project implementation and 
execution scrutiny
To give preferential treatment to certain contractors a .
items.explanation:
The Due Process policy aims to certify for public funding only those projects and 
contracts that pass proper project implementation and execution scrutiny.
390

items.question:
Who is responsible for the overall monitoring and oversight of public procurement under the 
Public Procurement Act 2007?
options: The National Council on Public Procurement and the Bureau of Public Procurement
items.explanation:
The National Council on Public Procurement and the Bureau of Public 
Procurement are the regulatory authorities responsible for the monitoring and oversight of public 
procurement.

items.question:
What is one of the ways the Bureau of Public Procurement helps to prevent fraudulent 
procurement?
options: By preventing all foreign companies from bidding
By applying the provisions of the Public Procurement Act where necessary
By keeping all procurement information secret
By allowing only politically connected individuals to bidQ .
items.explanation:
A function of the Bureau is to prevent fraudulent and unfair procurement and, 
where necessary, apply the provisions of the Act.

items.question:
The Bureau of Public Procurement is tasked with organizing what for procurement 
professionals?
items.explanation:
A function of the Bureau is to organize training and development programmes for 
procurement professionals.

items.question:
What must a project or contract adhere to under the Due Process policy to be funded by the 
government?
options:
An internationally acceptable bidding system of openness, competition, cost accuracy, and 
proper procedure
A bidding system based on personal relationships
A system that prioritizes speed over accuracya .
items.explanation:
Any project or contract to be funded by the government must adhere strictly to an
391
internationally acceptable bidding system of openness, competition, cost accuracy, and proper 
procedure.

items.question:
What is the purpose of the Bureau's review of the procurement and award of contract 
procedures of every entity to which the Act applies?
options: To reduce the number of entities
To increase the cost of contracts
To ensure compliance with the Act
items.explanation:
The Bureau's function is to review the procurement and award of contract 
procedures of every entity to which the Act applies.

items.question:
According to the document, what is the role of the Bureau of Public Procurement in the 
process of contract awards?
options: It certifies federal procurement prior to the award of contracts.
It sets the final price for all contracts.
It only monitors projects after they have been awarded.a .
items.explanation:
A main function of the Bureau is to "subject to thresholds as may be set by the 
Council, certify Federal procurement prior to the award of contract".

items.question: What is a key function of the Bureau regarding major contracts?
options: To keep them secret from the public
To publish their details in the procurement journal
To award them to the highest bidder
To allow Ministries to handle them independentlyQ .
items.explanation:
A function of the Bureau is to publish the details of major contracts in the 
procurement journal.

items.question:
The Due Process policy aims to prevent extra-budgetary spending in MDAs by ensuring 
what?
options: Only projects with due appropriation by the National Assembly are certified and funded
All MDAs can spend as much as they want
Funds are sourced from international donors only

Projects are funded even without a budget
□ .
items.explanation:
The policy aims to prevent extra-budgetary spending by ensuring that only
392
projects with due appropriation by the National Assembly are certified and funded for execution.

items.question: What is the implication of the Due Process policy on projects funded by the government?
options: They must prioritize speed over procedure.
They must follow laid-down rules and regulations.
They are not subject to any form of oversight.
They are awarded based on the discretion of a single individual.a .
items.explanation:
Due Process ensures that government business is carried out on the basis of 
relevant and prescribed laws, rules, regulations, and practices.

items.question:
What is the ultimate purpose of the Due Process policy's objective to guarantee the 
elimination of waste?
options: To prolong the duration of projects
items.explanation:
The objective to eliminate waste is directly linked to the aim of achieving value for 
money.

items.question:
Who is the regulatory authority responsible for setting standards and developing the legal 
framework for public procurement in Nigeria?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
items.explanation:
The Bureau of Public Procurement is one of the regulatory authorities responsible 
for monitoring and oversight, and for setting standards and developing the legal framework for 
public procurement.

items.question:
Which of the following is an objective of the Due Process policy related to project 
conceptualization?
options: To ensure projects are conceptualized on an ad-hoc basis
To ensure projects are disconnected from the annual financial appropriations

To ensure that project conceptualization and packaging match the defined priorities and 
targets as contained in the annual financial appropriations

To allow project conceptualization to be done by external consultants only
Q 393
items.explanation:
A key objective is to ensure that project conceptualization and packaging match 
the defined priorities and targets as contained in the annual financial appropriations.

items.question:
What does the Bureau of Public Procurement do with all federal procurement plans and 
information?
options: It collates and maintains them in an archival system.
It publishes them in a daily newspaper.
It sends them to the private sector.a .
items.explanation:
A function of the Bureau is to "collate and maintain in an archival system all 
federal procurement plans and information".

items.question:
The Public Procurement Act requires procurement to be conducted in a manner which is 
what?
items.explanation:
Procurement should be conducted in a manner that is "transparent, timely, and 
equitable for ensuring accountability and conformity with the Act and regulations deriving 
therefrom".
Based on the "Annual Defence of the Budget before the National Assembly" section of 
Chapter four of the Civil Service Handbook, here are five multiple-choice questions with 
answers and explanations.
Part 4: Civil Service Handbook: Annual Budget Defence

items.question:
Who is required to lead the delegation from a Ministry, Department, or Agency (MDA) to 
defend their budget estimates before the National Assembly?
options: The Minister or Chief Executive Officer
The Director of Finance and Accounts
items.explanation:
The Minister or Chief Executive Officer is expected to lead the delegation to the 
National Assembly to defend the budget estimates.

items.question: The budget defence session is designed to do what?
options: Scrutinize and justify the budget proposals for the MDA
Review the performance of past contracts
items.explanation:
The budget defence session is a platform for the National Assembly to scrutinize 
and justify the MDA's budget proposals, highlighting the need for funds and showing how they 
will be spent.

items.question: What is the purpose of preparing a "Fact Book" for the budget defence?
options: To serve as a personal diary for the Minister

To provide a comprehensive resource of information to answer any questions from the 
National Assembly
To be a list of all staff in the MDA
To track the progress of ongoing projects a .
items.explanation:
The "Fact Book" is a key document that contains all relevant information to 
address any queries from the National Assembly during the budget defence.

items.question:
Before presenting the budget to the National Assembly, an MDA must ensure that the 
proposed budget is in conformity with what?
options: The Federal Government's overall budget framework and policy thrust
items.explanation:
The MDA's budget proposals must be in line with the Federal Government's 
budget framework and policy thrust for the year.

items.question:
A successful budget defence is dependent on the ability of the MDA's delegation to do 
what?
options: Provide all information in a timely and effective manner
Promise to deliver on all projects, regardless of funding
items.explanation:
A successful budget defence hinges on the delegation's ability to provide all 
information requested by the National Assembly in a timely, effective, and convincing manner.
395
Section B
Part 1: Civil Service Handbook: Relations with National Assembly and Its Public 
Accounts Committees

items.question: What is the primary role of the Public Accounts Committees of the National Assembly?
options: To prepare the annual budget for Ministries, Departments, and Agencies (MDAs)
To manage and invest public funds

To scrutinize government expenditures based on the reports of the Auditor-General for the 
Federation
To approve all new government contracts a .
items.explanation:
The Public Accounts Committees scrutinize the reports of the Auditor-General for 
the Federation on the accounts of MDAs and call the accounting officers to explain any 
discrepancies.

items.question: Who is the Accounting Officer for a Ministry?
options: The Director of Finance and Accounts

The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
□ .
items.explanation:
The Permanent Secretary of a Ministry is designated as the Accounting Officer 
and is responsible for all expenditures and revenue.

items.question:
In the context of a Public Accounts Committee hearing, what is the role of the 
Accountant-General of the Federation?
options: To represent all MDAs and answer for them
To provide clarification on accounting procedures and financial regulations
items.explanation:
The Accountant-General of the Federation attends the committee's sittings and 
provides clarification on accounting procedures, treasury circulars, and financial regulations

items.question: What is the purpose of the audit query issued by the Auditor-General for the Federation?
options: To highlight any financial anomalies, unauthorized expenditures, or lack of proper accounting
items.explanation:
The Auditor-General's report is based on audit queries, which highlight financial
396
irregularities such as lack of proper accounting, unauthorized expenditures, and other financial 
anomalies.

items.question:
A Permanent Secretary, as an Accounting Officer, is expected to respond to audit queries 
within what timeframe?
items.explanation: The accounting officer has a period of 21 days to respond to an audit query.

items.question:
Which document is the primary basis for the scrutiny of government accounts by the Public 
Accounts Committees?
options: The Annual Report of the Auditor-General for the Federation
items.explanation:
The committees rely on the annual report of the Auditor-General for the 
Federation to scrutinize the accounts of MDAs.

items.question:
What is the consequence for an Accounting Officer who fails to appear before the Public 
Accounts Committee when summoned?
options: A penalty of two years' imprisonment
The committee can recommend sanctions, including dismissalQ .
items.explanation:
Failure of an accounting officer to appear before the committee when summoned 
is a serious offense, and the committee can recommend sanctions, including dismissal.

items.question:
To whom does a Permanent Secretary, as an Accounting Officer, submit their responses to 
audit queries?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation

The Director of Finance and Accounts
Q .
items.explanation:
The Accounting Officer's response to an audit query must be submitted to the
397
Auditor-General for the Federation for vetting before the Public Accounts Committee's sitting.

items.question: The Auditor-General's report on the accounts of the Federation is submitted to which body?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
items.explanation:
The Auditor-General submits their report on the accounts of the Federation to the 
National Assembly.

items.question: What is the purpose of a Treasury Circular in financial management?
options: To provide guidelines for international trade
To give instructions on accounting procedures, financial regulations, and rules
items.explanation:
Treasury Circulars are directives that provide clear instructions and guidelines on 
accounting procedures, financial regulations, and rules to all government agencies.
Part 2: Management of Relations with Parastatals

items.question: What is the primary role of a Parastatal?
options: To assist the Ministry in its policy formulation.
To manage all human resource issues for the Ministry.
To implement specific policies and programmes of the government as assigned.

To act as a legislative body for the Ministry.
□ .
items.explanation:
Parastatals are autonomous organizations, which exist to implement specific 
policies and programmes assigned to them by the government.

items.question: Who is the Accounting Officer of a Parastatal?
options: The Permanent Secretary of the supervising Ministry.
The Chairman of the Board of the Parastatal.
The Chief Executive/Director-General of the Parastatal.

The Minister of the supervising Ministry.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Chief Executive or Director-General of a parastatal is the Accounting Officer 
for all expenditures and revenue.

items.question:
The relationship between a Ministry and its Parastatal should be based on which of the
398
following principles?
options: A master-servant relationship where the Ministry dictates all activities.
A relationship where the Parastatal has total autonomy with no Ministry oversight.

A relationship of constant conflict and competition.
□ .
items.explanation:
The relationship is expected to be based on mutual respect, understanding, and 
consultation, avoiding a master-servant dynamic.

items.question:
Which body is responsible for the appointment and removal of members of the Board of a 
Parastatal?
options: The Chief Executive of the Parastatal

The President or a delegated authority
□ .
items.explanation:
The President or a delegated authority appoints and removes the members of the 
Board of a Parastatal.

items.question: What is the role of the Permanent Secretary in relation to a Parastatal?
options: To be the Accounting Officer of the Parastatal.
To manage the day-to-day operations of the Parastatal.
To ensure the Parastatal's policies align with government policies.

To audit the financial accounts of the Parastatal.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Permanent Secretary's role includes ensuring that the Parastatal's activities 
and policies are consistent with government policy and that the Parastatal submits its budgets 
and reports on time.

items.question: The Chief Executive of a Parastatal is expected to attend which of the following without fail?
options: The meetings of the Public Accounts Committees of the National Assembly

All social functions of the Ministry
□ .
items.explanation:
The Chief Executive, as the accounting officer of the Parastatal, must attend the 
meetings of the Public Accounts Committees of the National Assembly when summoned.

items.question: Who is responsible for providing policy direction and overall supervision to a Parastatal?
options:
The Permanent Secretary of the supervising Ministry.
399
The Chief Executive of the Parastatal.
The Minister of the supervising Ministry.

The Chairman of the Board.
Q .
items.explanation:
The Minister, as the political head of the Ministry, provides policy direction and 
overall supervision to the Parastatal.

items.question: The Chief Executive of a Parastatal is appointed by whom?
items.explanation: The President appoints the Chief Executive of a Parastatal.

items.question: A key function of the Board of a Parastatal is to do what?
options: Manage the day-to-day activities of the Parastatal.
Formulate policies for the Parastatal in line with the government's overall policy.
Approve all government contracts above a certain threshold.

Recruit and train all staff of the Parastatal.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Board of a Parastatal formulates policies that are in harmony with the 
Ministry's and government's overall policies.

items.question: What is the role of the Chairman of the Board of a Parastatal?
options: To be the Accounting Officer of the Parastatal.
To supervise the day-to-day running of the Parastatal.
To preside over all Board meetings and ensure order.
To represent the Parastatal in all international forums.a .
items.explanation:
The Chairman is responsible for presiding over all Board meetings and ensuring 
that discussions are focused and orderly.

items.question: Who provides the necessary infrastructure, logistics, and personnel for a Board's sittings?
options: The Office of the Head of Civil Service of the Federation
items.explanation:
The Parastatal's management is responsible for providing the necessary 
infrastructure, logistics, and personnel for the Board's sittings.

items.question:
The relationship between the Board of a Parastatal and the Chief Executive should be one 
of what?
options: Subordination of the Board to the Chief Executive.

A relationship where the Board dictates every action of the Chief Executive.
□ .
items.explanation:
The relationship between the Board and the Chief Executive should be cordial, 
based on mutual respect and collaboration.

items.question: The Chief Executive of a Parastatal is responsible for what aspect of the organization?
options:
Approval of the Parastatal's budget.
□ . Day-to-day management and administration.
items.explanation:
The Chief Executive is responsible for the day-to-day management and 
administration of the Parastatal.

items.question: What is a key responsibility of the Chief Executive of a Parastatal to the Board?
options: To implement the decisions and policies of the Board.
To refuse to implement decisions that are not to his liking.
To only report to the Minister.

To manage the Board's personal affairs.
□ ..
items.explanation:
The Chief Executive is expected to implement the decisions and policies of the 
Board.

items.question:
What happens if there is a conflict between the Chief Executive and the Board of a 
Parastatal?
options: The Chief Executive should immediately resign.
The Board should dissolve the management.
The supervising Minister should be involved to resolve the matter.
items.explanation:
Where there are conflicts, the supervising Minister should be brought in to 
mediate and resolve the matter amicably.
401

items.question: Which of the following is a function of the Board of a Parastatal concerning personnel?
options: To manage the day-to-day recruitment of junior staff.
To approve the appointment and promotion of staff.
To be the sole disciplinary body for all staff.

To manage the payroll and salaries.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Board is responsible for the approval of the appointment and promotion of 
staff.

items.question: The Board of a Parastatal is expected to be financially responsible for what?
options: The day-to-day expenses of the Parastatal.
The honorarium and allowances of the Board members.
The preparation of the Parastatal's budget.
The management of the Parastatal's bank accounts.a ..
items.explanation:
The Board is expected to prepare the Parastatal's budget for submission to the 
National Assembly through the supervising Ministry.

items.question:
The Chief Executive of a Parastatal is expected to submit a report to the Board at what 
frequency?
items.explanation: The Chief Executive is expected to submit a report to the Board at every quarter.

items.question: What is the purpose of the Board of a Parastatal?
options: To act as an advisory body only.
To provide a platform for political patronage.
To give direction and provide oversight to the Parastatal.
items.explanation:
The Board's primary purpose is to provide policy direction and oversight to the 
Parastatal, ensuring it fulfills its mandate.

items.question:
The relationship between a Minister and a Parastatal is expected to be based on which of 
the following?
options: A relationship of complete control by the Minister.

A relationship between an adviser and a client.
402
A relationship of coordination and supervision.
A relationship between a superior and a subordinate.Q .
items.explanation:
The relationship between a Minister and a Parastatal is one of coordination, 
supervision, and policy direction.
Part 3: The Civil Service

items.question: What is considered a "critical success factor" for the government to achieve its objectives?
options: A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service.
items.explanation:
A professional, competent, and result-oriented Civil Service is a critical success 
factor for the government to realize its objectives.

items.question:
The 1999 Constitution defines the "Civil Service of the Federation" as service in a civil 
capacity as staff of which of the following?
options: A Ministry or department of the Government of the Federation.
items.explanation:
The 1999 Constitution defines the Civil Service of the Federation as service in a 
civil capacity as staff of the Office of the President, the Vice-President, a Ministry, or a 
department of the government.

items.question:
In contrast to the Civil Service, what does the 1999 Constitution define the "Public Service of 
the Federation" as?
options: Service of the Federation in any capacity in respect of the Government of the Federation.
The legislative arm of government only.
items.explanation:
The 1999 Constitution defines the public service of the Federation as the service 
of the Federation in any capacity in respect of the Government of the Federation.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental role of the Public Service?
options: To serve the public and the nation.
To effectively promote a political party's agenda.

To deliver services to meet the needs of the citizenry.
Q .
items.explanation:
The fundamental roles of the Public Service are to serve the public, the nation, 
and the community, and also, to effectively promote the common good.

items.question:
What is a key characteristic that distinguishes the Civil Service from elected officials like 
members of the National Assembly?
options: Civil Servants are not paid salaries.
The Civil Service enjoys continuity of existence.
Civil Servants are limited to a short term of office.

The Civil Service has no pool of experience.
a .
items.explanation:
The Civil Service is a body or organ which enjoys continuity of existence, unlike 
elected officials who have limited terms of office.

items.question:
The Civil Service is described as the "instrument of the Government of the day," but it is not 
what?
options: The instrument of the political party in power.

The instrument of the Judiciary.
a .
items.explanation:
The Civil Service is the instrument of the Government of the day, but neither the 
instrument of a political party nor of a trade union.

items.question: What is a core principle in the discharge of the Civil Service's functions?
items.explanation:
The principles in the discharge of the Civil Service's functions include 
accountability, responsiveness, courtesy, objectivity, impartiality, and upholding the public trust 
at all levels.

items.question:
The Civil Service is divided into Ministries and Extra-Ministerial Departments to facilitate 
what?
options:
Assignment of responsibilities for specific activities
404
items.explanation:
In order to facilitate the assignment of responsibilities for a group of specific 
activities, the Civil Service is divided into Ministries and Extra-Ministerial Departments.

items.question: The political head of a Ministry is the Minister, but who is the administrative head?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
items.explanation:
The Minister is the political head of the Ministry, and the Permanent Secretary 
serves as the administrative head.

items.question: Who is the Accounting Officer of a Ministry?
options: The Director of Finance and Accounts

The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation
□ .
items.explanation: The Permanent Secretary is the Ministry's Accounting Officer.

items.question:
The Permanent Secretary of a Ministry is directly responsible for all of the following 
EXCEPT:
options: Formulating government policy on behalf of the Minister.Q ..
items.explanation:
The Permanent Secretary is not responsible for formulating government policy. 
Instead, they are responsible for ensuring the implementation of decisions, maintaining 
discipline, preventing fraud, and serving as the administrative head, among other duties.

items.question:
Below the Permanent Secretary, who heads the Departments and is directly responsible to 
the Permanent Secretary?
items.explanation:
Below the Permanent Secretary are the other cadres of Civil Servants. Directors 
head the Departments and are directly responsible to the Permanent Secretary.

items.question: The current integrated structure of the Federal Civil Service is likened to what shape?
items.explanation:
The integrated structure which exists in the Federal Civil Service as of today is 
like a pyramid.

items.question: The Administrative and Professional Officers' duties are derived from what?
options: The directives of the Permanent Secretary.
items.explanation:
The raison d'etre and duties of the Administrative and the Professional Officers 
derive from our system of Government.

items.question: What is the purpose of the Administrative and Professional Officers?
options: To make policy decisions for the Minister.
To advise on appropriate decisions and subsequently put them into effect.
To manage the finances of the Ministry.
To handle all legal matters of the Ministry.Q .
items.explanation:
It is for this group to bring together the many and diverse views involved in 
matters requiring policy decisions, to advise on the appropriate decisions and subsequently to 
put them into effect.

items.question:
Which of the following is a role of the Permanent Secretary, in relation to other government 
bodies?
options:
A memorandum to the Executive Council of the Federation for funds may have to be cleared 
with the Ministry of Finance.
Directly controlling the National Planning Commission.a .
items.explanation:
The Permanent Secretary in a Ministry may need to clear a memorandum for
406
funds with the Ministry of Finance before it is sent to the Executive Council of the Federation.

items.question: The Civil Service is based on the principle of anonymity, which means what?
options: Civil servants do not have to identify themselves.
The Civil Servant is not held personally responsible for the Minister's decisions.

Civil servants do not have to sign official documents.
¡3 .
items.explanation:
The principle of anonymity of civil servants implies that the Permanent Secretary, 
in giving advice to the Minister, does not do so in his name but rather as an official. Once a 
Minister takes a decision, the civil servant is not held personally responsible for it.

items.question: What is the purpose of the principle of neutrality in the Civil Service?
options: To allow civil servants to participate in partisan politics.
To ensure that civil servants serve the government of the day without political bias.
To prevent civil servants from having any opinions.
To allow civil servants to publicly criticize government policies, a .
items.explanation:
The principle of neutrality ensures that civil servants do not take part in partisan 
politics, serving the government of the day without political bias.

items.question: How does the Civil Service ensure its permanence?
options: By ensuring that all civil servants have a life-long appointment.
By maintaining a pool of experience and know-how.
By having a constitutionally guaranteed term of office.

By appointing civil servants based on their political affiliation.
Q .
items.explanation:
The permanence of the civil service is maintained through the collective 
experience and know-how of its members.

items.question: The Udoji Public Service Review Commission in 1972 was primarily focused on what?
options: Reducing the size of the Civil Service.
Increasing efficiency and effectiveness in the Public Service.
Limiting the powers of the Civil Service.
items.explanation:
The Udoji Commission focused primarily on the larger issue of increasing 
efficiency and effectiveness in the Public Service.

items.question:
What was one of the key recommendations of the Udoji Commission?
407
options: The separation of Administrative and Professional Officers.
The introduction of the Open Reporting System for performance evaluation.
The creation of a separate grading system for each Ministry.
items.explanation:
The Commission introduced the Open Reporting System for performance 
evaluation, the Unified Grading and Salary Structure, and the creation of the Unified Senior 
Management Group.

items.question:
Which of the following was a prominent undesirable characteristic of the Nigerian Civil 
Service in the late 1980s?
items.explanation:
In the late 1980s, the Civil Service had several undesirable characteristics, 
including over-centralization, incessant conflicts among cadres, and little emphasis on results.

items.question: What was a key feature of the Civil Service Reforms of 1988?
options: The creation of more departments in each Ministry.

The appointment of the Minister as the Accounting Officer and Chief Executive of the 
Ministry.
The introduction of a unified grading system.
The abolition of the Permanent Secretary position.3 .
items.explanation:
The 1988 reforms introduced the concept of the Minister being the Accounting 
Officer and Chief Executive of the Ministry, with the Permanent Secretary being a senior career 
officer who served as a support staff.

items.question: One of the limitations of the 1988 Civil Service Reforms was what?
options: Strong commitment from top government functionaries.
Absence of human resistance to change.
Inadequate knowledge about the provisions of the Reforms.Q .
items.explanation:
One of the limitations was inadequate knowledge about the provisions of the 
Reforms on the part of many Civil Servants and top Government functionaries.

items.question:
What was the key objective of the Public Service Reform Programme of 1999?
408
options: To reduce the size of the Civil Service.
To create a modern, professional, and efficient Civil Service.
To increase the number of political appointees.
To introduce a new salary structure.3 .
items.explanation:
The Public Service Reform Programme of 1999 was initiated to reposition the 
Civil Service to be a modern, professional, and efficient institution.

items.question: What is the purpose of the "golden handshake" scheme for retiring public servants?
options: To prepare affected staff for new vocations after retirement.
To offer them a new job.
items.explanation:
The "golden handshake" scheme provided orientation for individual development 
and empowerment to prepare affected staff for new vocations after retirement.

items.question: According to the handbook, what are the three arms of Government?
options: Public Service, Civil Service, and Armed Forces.3 .
items.explanation:
Government is made up of three arms, viz; the Legislature, the Executive and the 
Judiciary.

items.question: What is the primary responsibility of the Executive arm of government?
items.explanation:
The Executive is responsible for the day-to-day management of Government, 
including conceiving, formulating, executing, and monitoring policies.

items.question: The law making power of the government at the Federal level is vested in which body?
items.explanation:
The law making power of Government is vested in the National Assembly, at the 
Federal level.

items.question: Which tier of government is described as being "closest to the people"?
items.explanation:
Local Government Chairmen and Councilors are widely regarded as being closest 
to the people.

items.question: The topmost career Civil Servant in a Local Government is designated as what?
items.explanation:
The topmost career Civil Servant in a Local Government set-up is designated as 
Secretary.

items.question: The Federal Executive Council, or Cabinet, has the duty and responsibility of doing what?
options:
Passing laws.
Q Correct Answer:: B.
items.explanation:
The Executive Council of the Federation, otherwise known as the Federal 
Executive Council or the Cabinet, has the duty and responsibility of determining Government 
policies on various matters.

items.question: Who is the Secretary to the Federal Executive Council?
options: The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation.
The Secretary to the Government of the Federation.
items.explanation:
The Secretary to the Council is the Secretary to the Government of the
410
Federation.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the Public Service, according to the document?
items.explanation:
Apart from the Civil Service, the Public Service also encompasses the Armed 
Forces, the Judiciary, the Legislature, and the Police, as well as Parastatals, Statutory 
Agencies, and Government-owned companies.

items.question: Good governance is increasingly accepted as an important fundamental for what?
items.explanation:
Good governance is being increasingly accepted as an important fundamental for 
efficient economic management and development.

items.question:
Which characteristic of good governance means that decisions are taken and enforced in a 
manner that follows rules and regulations, and information is freely available?
items.explanation:
Transparency means that decisions taken and their enforcement are done in a 
manner that follows rules and regulations, and information is freely available and accessible.

items.question:
Which characteristic of good governance requires that institutions and processes try to 
serve all stakeholders within a reasonable timeframe?
items.explanation:
Responsiveness means that institutions and processes try to serve all 
stakeholders within a reasonable timeframe.
411

items.question: What is a key role of the Civil Service in the context of good governance?
options: To act as an opposition to the government.

To ensure that Nigeria is administered in a manner consistent with the attributes of good 
governance.
items.explanation:
The Civil Service has a crucial role to play in ensuring that Nigeria is administered 
in a manner consistent with the aforementioned attributes and characteristics of good 
governance.

items.question:
Which reform introduced the concept that Permanent Secretaries were no longer the 
Accounting Officers of Ministries?
options: The Udoji Public Service Review Commission of 1972.
The Public Service Reform Programme of 1999.
items.explanation:
The 1988 reforms made the Minister the Accounting Officer, and the Permanent 
Secretary was relegated to a senior career officer who supported the Minister.

items.question: What was a key limitation of the 1988 Civil Service Reforms?
options: The reforms were too simple to be implemented.
The reforms were too popular with the public.

There was an absence of strong commitment from top government functionaries due to a 
misconception that it would erode their powers.

There was too much funding for the reforms.
Q .
items.explanation:
There was an absence of strong commitment on the part of top Government 
functionaries to the implementation of the Reforms partly as a result of misconception that full 
implementation would erode their powers.

items.question: The "golden handshake" was part of which reform?
options: The Udoji Public Service Review Commission.
The Public Service Reform Programme of 1999.
items.explanation:
As part of the Public Service Reform Programme of 1999, a "golden handshake" 
scheme was provided.
412

items.question: The Public Service Reform Programme of 1999 was a response to what?
options: The need to reposition the Civil Service to be more efficient and professional.
The need to reduce government spending.
items.explanation:
The reform programme was initiated to create a modern, professional, and 
efficient Civil Service.

items.question: Who is the Chief Adviser to the Minister in a Ministry?
options: The Director of Finance and Accounts.
The Head of the Civil Service of the Federation.a .
items.explanation: The Minister's Chief Adviser is the Permanent Secretary.

items.question: What does the term "Parastatals" refer to in the context of the Public Service?
options: Only the Armed Forces and the Police.
items.explanation:
The Public Service includes Parastatals, Statutory Agencies and 
Government-owned companies..

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an attribute of good governance mentioned in the handbook?
items.explanation:
The handbook lists attributes of good governance, including Rule of Law, 
Participation, Transparency, Accountability, Effectiveness, Efficiency, Equity, Inclusiveness, 
Consensus Orientation, Responsiveness, Security and Order, Conflicts, Purposeful Leadership, 
and Welfare of People, Political patronage is not listed.

items.question:
What is the term for a financial report that shows the income and expenditure of a 
government body for a specific period?
items.explanation:
The Statement of Financial Performance shows the income and expenditure for a 
particular period.

items.question: What is the purpose of the capital budget?
options: To fund staff salaries and wages.
To fund capital projects and investments like infrastructure.
items.explanation:
The capital budget is a financial plan for the acquisition of assets, such as 
infrastructure, equipment, and other investments.

items.question:
Who is responsible for the proper custody of public funds, stamps, and other government 
documents in a Ministry, Department, or Agency (MDA)?
options: The Director of Finance and Accounts.
items.explanation:
The Director of Finance and Accounts is responsible for the proper custody of all 
public funds, stamps, and other government documents.

items.question: What is the primary role of the Auditor-General for the Federation?
options: To manage all government bank accounts.
To check and report on government expenditures and public accounts.

To control the release of funds to MDAs.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Auditor-General is the Head of the Supreme Audit Institution of the country 
and is responsible for auditing and reporting on the public accounts of the Federation.

items.question:
The Civil Service is described as a "body or organ which enjoys continuity of existence" 
because its members:
options: Are not limited to a short term of office.
Are not elected by the people.
items.explanation:
The Civil Service is a body or organ which enjoys continuity of existence. Its 
members, unlike members of the National Assembly or a House of Assembly, are not limited to 
a short term of office at the end of which they may or may not be returned to office46.
Part 4:Management of Human Resources in the Civil Service

items.question: What is the generally accepted definition of "management" as an activity?
options: The process of making financial decisions for an organization.

The process of getting things done effectively and efficiently with and through other people to 
achieve organizational objectives.
The process of supervising subordinates and assigning tasks.

The process of formulating policies for the government.
E3 .
items.explanation:
Many writers define management as the process of getting things done effectively 
and efficiently with and through other people to achieve the objectives of an organization.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a basic function of management in the document?
items.explanation:
While budgeting is mentioned as a very important function for an organization, the 
text does not list it as a "basic function" of management in the same way it lists planning, 
organizing, directing, staffing, coordinating, and reporting

items.question: What does the function of "Planning" primarily involve?
options: Establishing organizational objectives and determining plans to accomplish them.

Ensuring that various branches of the organization work smoothly.
Q .
items.explanation:
Planning involves establishing organizational objectives and determining the 
plans to accomplish those goals.

items.question:
"Organizing" in management is the process of developing a structure or framework among 
personnel, functions, and physical factors. Which of the following is a key part of this process?
options:
Allocating inputs (human and economic resources) to the various organizational units.
415
Recognizing that plans may require readjustment.

Keeping appropriate authorities informed of what is going on.
Q .
items.explanation:
The organizing function involves determining the organizational structure and 
allocating inputs, such as human and economic resources, to the various units.

items.question: The function of "Directing" involves all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Ensuring staff motivation for high performance.

Hiring the right individuals for the appropriate jobs.
Answer: D.
items.explanation:
Directing involves disseminating instructions, providing leadership, and ensuring 
staff motivation. Hiring individuals is part of the "Staffing" function.

items.question: What is the purpose of the "Staffing" function in management?
options: To keep appropriate authorities informed through reporting and research.
To ensure the viability of the organization through careful budgeting.

To put in place an efficient system of recruitment by hiring the right individuals and providing 
training.

To check progress against plans and modify them based on feedback.
a .
items.explanation:
Staffing is the personnel function concerned with putting in place an efficient 
system of recruitment by hiring the right individuals for the appropriate jobs, job analyzing, 
training, and providing favorable conditions of work.

items.question:
Human Resources Management (HRM) is defined as the part of management that provides 
for the effective utilization of human resources to achieve what?
options: The objectives of the organization and the satisfaction and development of employees.
The financial goals of the organization only.
The political objectives of the government.

The personal goals of the managers.
□ .
items.explanation:
HRM provides for the effective utilization of human resources to achieve both the 
objectives of the organization and the satisfaction and development of employees.

items.question: All of the following are listed as areas covered by Human Resources Management EXCEPT:
items.explanation:
HRM covers human resource planning, job analysis, staffing, training and 
development, compensation and benefits, performance appraisal and management, industrial 
relations, staff welfare, and disengagement. Public relations and marketing are not mentioned.

items.question:
A key objective of HRM is to enhance the capabilities of employees and ensure they 
possess the requisite level of competence, knowledge, skill, and attitude to do what?
options: Reduce the size of the Civil Service.
items.explanation:
It is necessary to enhance the capabilities of employees and ensure that they 
possess the requisite level of competence, knowledge, skill, and attitude, which must be 
continually improved upon to increase productivity.

items.question:
Which of the following is an HRM challenge that has been evident in the Civil Service over 
the years?
options: Availability of reliable data on training needs.
Uncoordinated and fragmented approaches to human resources development.

Adequate linkages between training output and the duty post.
□ .
items.explanation:
The challenges include under-utilization and non-utilization of human resources, 
uncoordinated and fragmented approaches to human resources development, and 
uncoordinated training programmes.

items.question: A challenge in the Civil Service's HRM is the inability to do what with skilled manpower?
items.explanation:
Challenges include the inability to recruit and retain the needed well-trained and 
skilled manpower.

items.question: A key challenge related to the recruitment process in the Civil Service is the perception that:
options: It is too transparent and equitable.

The recruitment process and advancement procedure need to be reviewed to ensure equity
417
and transparency.
User agencies play dominant roles in the recruitment of senior officers.
The Federal Character principle is being applied correctly.Q .
items.explanation:
There is a perception that both the recruitment process and the advancement 
procedure need to be reviewed to ensure equity and transparency.

items.question: The existence of "ghost workers" is symptomatic of what problem in the Civil Service?
options: Poor personnel records and a flawed payroll control system.
Inadequate linkages between training and duty posts.
items.explanation:
The existence of ghost workers is symptomatic of poor personnel records and a 
flawed payroll control system, particularly in MDAs not yet part of the IPPIS programme.

items.question:
What is described as a major cause for all the reform efforts in the Civil Service over the 
years?
options: The assiduous efforts of the government to overcome HRM challenges.
items.explanation:
The government's assiduous efforts to overcome these challenges have been the 
major reason for all the reform efforts in the Civil Service over the years.

items.question:
Which two key institutions have oversight responsibilities for policy articulation and 
implementation, as well as quality assurance on Human Resources issues?
options: The National Assembly and the Judiciary.

The Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF) and the Federal Civil 
Service Commission (FCSC).
The Ministry of Finance and the Budget Office of the Federation.
The National Council on Establishments (NCE) and the National Planning Commission, a .
items.explanation:
The key institutions with oversight responsibilities for HR issues are the Office of 
the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF) and the Federal Civil Service 
Commission (FCSC).

items.question:
What is the purpose of the National Council on Establishments (NCE), which has been in 
existence since 1957?
418
options: To manage the careers of all civil servants.

For experience sharing and harmonization of human resource issues between Federal and 
State Governments.
To handle all disciplinary cases for federal staff.
To recruit all new employees into the civil service.a .
items.explanation:
The National Council on Establishments has been in existence since 1957 for 
experience sharing, alignment, and harmonization of human resource issues between the 
Federal and the State Governments.

items.question:
What is the Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF) responsible 
for?
options: Disciplinary control of all civil servants.
General supervision of the affairs of the Civil Service.
items.explanation: The OHCSF provides general supervision of the affairs of the Civil Service.

items.question: The Management Services Office (MSO) within the OHCSF deals with issues of:
options:
Systems, structures, work processes, and other related matters for ensuring the efficiency of 
the Service.
Coordinating service-wide administration of training and development.a .
items.explanation:
The Management Services Office deals with issues of systems, structures, work 
processes, and other related matters for ensuring the efficiency and effectiveness of the 
Service.

items.question:
The Establishments and Pensions Office (EPO) is responsible for all of the following 
EXCEPT:
options: Being the custodian of service records.
items.explanation:
The EPO is the custodian of Service records and approved establishments and is 
responsible for industrial relations and pension administration. Coordinating training is the 
responsibility of the Manpower Development Office.
419

items.question: The Manpower Development Office (MDO) has the responsibility for coordinating what?
options: The pool system for senior officers.
Service-wide administration of training and development.
Preparation of draft Council memoranda on HRM matters.
items.explanation:
The MDO has the responsibility for ensuring the Service has its fair share of 
quality manpower and in this context, the Office coordinates Service-wide administration of 
training and development.
420
Section C
Pat 1: Management of Human Resources in the Civil Service

items.question: What is the purpose of the Career Management Office (CMO)?
options: To issue and revise Schemes of Service.
To prepare draft Council memoranda on HRM matters.
To manage the pool system for senior officers on Grade Levels 14-17.
items.explanation:
The Career Management Office manages the pool system, which involves 
managing the careers of officers at the senior management level (GLs.14-17).

items.question:
Who has the constitutional power to appoint persons to offices in the Federal Civil Service 
and exercise disciplinary control over them?
options: The Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation (OHCSF).
items.explanation:
The FCSC is the constitutional body that has the power to appoint persons to 
offices in the Federal Civil Service and exercise disciplinary control over them.

items.question: What is a function of the Federal Civil Service Commission (FCSC)?
options:
Serving as an appellate body for petitions from officers who feel aggrieved on appointment, 
promotion, and discipline matters.
Managing the day-to-day operations of Ministries.

Approving the budget of MDAs.
Q .
items.explanation:
The FCSC serves as an appellate body in respect of petitions from Officers in the 
MDAs who feel aggrieved concerning appointment, promotion, and discipline matters.

items.question: The Schemes of Service are issued and revised to indicate which of the following?
options: The number of staff to be recruited each year.

The correct nomenclature, salary grade levels, qualifications for entry, and schedule of duties 
for each cadre.
The disciplinary procedures for all civil servants.

The amount of pension to be paid to retired officers.
□ .
items.explanation:
The Schemes of Service indicate the correct nomenclature, the lowest to the 
highest Salary Grade Levels, qualifications for entry and progression within the cadre, and the
421
schedule of duties at each Grade Level.

items.question: What is a key role of the professional and line Ministries in the Civil Service?
options: Managing the careers of senior officers.
Formulating policies on HR for the entire Service.

The career development of their professional and technical staff below the senior 
management level.

Handling all recruitment for the Federal Civil Service.
□ .
items.explanation:
The professional and line Ministries are responsible for the career development of 
their professional and technical staff below the senior management level.

items.question: An officer can only claim to be acting in a superior post if which condition is met?
options: They have been asked to cover the schedule of the superior officer.
They have served in the post for more than 6 months.
The acting appointment has been expressly approved in writing by the constituted authority.

Their Permanent Secretary gives a verbal approval.
□
items.explanation:
For an officer to be able to claim to be acting on a particular post, such an 
arrangement must be expressly approved in writing by the constituted authority.

items.question: What is the purpose of preparing a "Fact Book" for the budget defence?
options: To serve as a personal diary for the Minister.

To provide a comprehensive resource of information to answer any questions from the 
National Assembly.
To be a list of all staff in the MDA.

To track the progress of ongoing projects.
Q .
items.explanation:
The "Fact Book" is a key document that contains all relevant information to address 
any queries from the National Assembly during the budget defence. This question is from a 
previous chapter but is included as it is a common question within the quiz context.

items.question:
Who is responsible for promoting good relations between Ministers and other political 
appointees, on the one hand, and Permanent Secretaries and other Civil Servants on the other 
hand?
options: The Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation.
items.explanation:
One of the functions of the OHCSF is promoting good relations between Ministers 
and other political appointees, on the one hand, and Permanent Secretaries and other Civil 
Servants on the other hand.

items.question: The Civil Service is based on the principle of anonymity, which means what?
options: Civil servants do not have to identify themselves.
The Civil Servant is not held personally responsible for the Minister's decisions.

Civil servants do not have to sign official documents.
□ .
items.explanation:
The principle of anonymity of civil servants implies that the Permanent Secretary, 
in giving advice to the Minister, does not do so in his name but rather as an official. Once a 
Minister takes a decision, the civil servant is not held personally responsible for it.

items.question: The principle of neutrality in the Civil Service ensures that civil servants do what?
options: Serve the government of the day without political bias.
Do not have any personal opinions.
items.explanation:
The principle of neutrality ensures that civil servants do not take part in partisan 
politics, serving the government of the day without political bias.

items.question: What was a key feature of the Civil Service Reforms of 1988?
options: The creation of more departments in each Ministry.

The appointment of the Minister as the Accounting Officer and Chief Executive of the 
Ministry.
The introduction of a unified grading system.
The abolition of the Permanent Secretary position.a .
items.explanation:
The 1988 reforms introduced the concept of the Minister being the Accounting 
Officer and Chief Executive of the Ministry, with the Permanent Secretary being a senior career 
officer who served as a support staff.

items.question: The "golden handshake" scheme was provided to prepare affected staff for what?
options: A new job in another ministry.
items.explanation:
The "golden handshake" scheme provided orientation for individual development 
and empowerment to prepare affected staff for new vocations after retirement.

items.question:
Who is responsible for ensuring that the Civil Service is administered in a manner consistent 
with the attributes of good governance?
items.explanation:
The Civil Service has a crucial role to play in ensuring that Nigeria is administered 
in a manner consistent with the aforementioned attributes and characteristics of good 
governance.

items.question: What is the purpose of preparing a "Fact Book" for the budget defence?
options: To serve as a personal diary for the Minister.
